pairs.question: What class of battleship did HMS Queen belong to, and how did it differ from the preceding Formidable-class?
pairs.answer: HMS Queen belonged to the London class of pre-dreadnought battleships. The Londons were near repeats of the Formidable-class battleships but featured modified armour protection.

pairs.question: What were the specifications of HMS Queen's main battery and top speed?
pairs.answer: HMS Queen's main battery consisted of four 12-inch (305-mm) guns, and she had a top speed of 18 knots (33 km/h; 21 mph).

pairs.question: Describe the construction timeline of HMS Queen from keel laying to completion.
pairs.answer: HMS Queen was laid down in March 1901, launched in March 1902, and completed in March 1904.

pairs.question: What were the major naval fleets and squadrons HMS Queen served in during her career?
pairs.answer: HMS Queen served with the Mediterranean Fleet (1904–1906, 1906–1908), the Atlantic Fleet, the Home Fleet, and the 5th Battle Squadron of the Second Fleet (1914).

pairs.question: What role did HMS Queen play in the Dardanelles Campaign during World War I?
pairs.answer: HMS Queen participated in the Landing at Cape Helles on 25–26 April 1915 and supported ANZAC forces ashore over the following month.

pairs.question: Why was HMS Queen converted into a depot ship in 1917, and what changes were made to her armament?
pairs.answer: HMS Queen was converted into a depot ship to support the Otranto Barrage. She was disarmed over the course of 1917 as part of this conversion.

pairs.question: What ultimately happened to HMS Queen after World War I, and when was she scrapped?
pairs.answer: After the war, HMS Queen was briefly used as a barracks ship in Britain. She was sold for scrap in 1920 and broken up the following year.
